spec_match.rationale: OPML import via DOMParser + JS recursion is a legitimate XSLT-replacement pattern; but the page never references XSLT directly or shows the equivalent XSL stylesheet for contrast, so the spec connection is implicit. CLAUDE.md's deprecation guidance asks for a working example of the recommended replacement — this is one, just not framed against the thing it replaces.

interactive.rationale: Drag-and-drop file zone, file input, sample button, JSON export, OPML re-export, tree render. 6 controls.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'drop your OPML file, get a structured outline tree, exportable as JSON or back as cleaned-up OPML'. Page delivers.

edge_cases.rationale: Detects duplicates (good), missing xmlUrl (good), parse errors. Doesn't handle nested outlines deeper than two levels gracefully in the stats; doesn't show what the original XSLT-based OPML viewer would have rendered.

references.rationale: See-also includes https://chromestatus.com/feature/4709671889534976 and the OPML spec link.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only; tree uses `<details><summary>` (good a11y) with --bg-stone keys.

runtime_evidence.rationale: Fresh Chrome DevTools MCP v152 sweep loaded the copied concept, exercised every detected control, found named controls, and reported no post-triage runtime/action failure.

conformance.rationale: A v152 suite now carries the same feature-ID/spec contract without modifying the immutable source-milestone suite.

openQuestions.title: Show the equivalent XSL stylesheet next to the JS
openQuestions.detail: Most OPML files in the wild used to be viewed with an `<?xml-stylesheet?>` PI pointing at an XSL stylesheet. Including the canonical OPML→HTML XSL alongside the JS walk function would teach migrators what they're replacing. Right now the page demonstrates the modern path but never references the old one.

openQuestions.title: Validate xmlUrl is actually a feed
openQuestions.detail: The page surfaces 'missing xmlUrl' but doesn't try to fetch the URLs that are present. A 'check feeds (X of Y reachable)' button that does HEAD requests through a CORS-proxy would add real value to the import flow — that's what someone moving subscriptions would actually want.

openQuestions.title: Handle deeply-nested outlines
openQuestions.detail: The walk function recurses into children but the rendered tree uses `<details>` at every nested level. Three or more levels deep stops looking like an outline. Either limit nesting in the visual or add a depth indicator.

openQuestions.title: Round-trip the OPML through the JSON form and back
openQuestions.detail: The page exports JSON and OPML, but doesn't let the user re-import their own JSON to verify the round-trip preserves the structure. A 'paste JSON' input would close that loop.

summary: Useful real-world OPML importer. Two recommendations to anchor it to the feature (XSLT removal): include the equivalent XSL stylesheet alongside the JS walker, and add a feed-reachability checker so the import flow has a payoff beyond pretty-printing the file.
